text: Course Name/Code: Systems Engineering/23CSPC307 Academic Year: 2025-26(even) Section: CSE3 Semester: VI Assignment -1 Case Study for Assignment A smart environmental monitoring system uses a wireless sensor network to collect data such as temperature, humidity, and air quality. Multiple sensors (S1-S10) are deployed in a region. These sensors communicate with a base station, which processes the da

nicate with a base station, which processes the data and sends it to the user application. The system supports two data acquisition methods: Push-based model -Sensors automatically send data to the base station when values change significantly. Pull-based model - The user sends a query to the base station, and the base station requests sensor data when needed. The system aims to reduce energy cons

when needed. The system aims to reduce energy consumption while providing accurate and timely data. Questions Explain the working of push-based and pull-based sensor data acquisition methods using the given case study. Compare push and pull models based on the following parameters: Data transfer Network traffic Latency Energy usage Use cases Design an EER (Enhanced Entity Relationship) diagram for

an EER (Enhanced Entity Relationship) diagram for the system showing entities such as: Sensor Sensor Network Base Station User Query Sensor Data Include relationships, attributes, and cardinalities. Identify the attributes for the main entities which is required to store the sensor data in a database. Draw the system architecture diagram showing: Sensor nodes Base station Communication network Us
Sensor nodes Base station Communication network User interface Discuss which model (push or pull) is more suitable for real-time monitoring applications and justify your answer.

CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 1 of 13Ø<ß CLIMATE SENTINEL A Global AI-Powered Climate Crisis Monitoring System Case Study for Assignment | Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Subject: Internet of Things / Distributed Systems | Level: Undergraduate / Postgraduate Background & Problem Statement Climate change is the most critica

oblem Statement Climate change is the most critical global challenge of the 21st century. Rising global temperatures, accelerating glacier melt, unpredictable extreme weather events, ocean acidification, prolonged droughts, and catastrophic wildfires are occurring simultaneously and at a pace that traditional monitoring infrastructure cannot adequately track. Conventional climate observation relie

tely track. Conventional climate observation relies on sparse weather stations, infrequent satellite passes, and manual field surveys - methods that are too slow, too coarse-grained, and too expensive to capture the fine-resolution, real-time data that scientists, governments, and disaster management agencies urgently need. CLIMATE SENTINEL addresses this gap by deploying a dense Wireless Sensor N

es this gap by deploying a dense Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) across six climate-critical zones - Arctic tundra, tropical rainforests, coastal deltas, high-altitude glaciers, semi-arid grasslands, and urban heat islands - to collect, transmit, and analyse multi- parameter environmental data continuously. System Overview The CLIMATE SENTINEL system deploys 120 heterogeneous sensor nodes (CS-01 thr

deploys 120 heterogeneous sensor nodes (CS-01 through CS- 120) distributed across six biomes. Each node is a solar-powered, low-power embedded device capable of measuring: - Atmospheric CO₂ concentration (ppm) and methane (ppb) - Surface and ambient temperature (°C) - Relative humidity (%) - Air Quality Index (PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, O₃) - Sea-level pressure and tidal gauge data - Glacier mass balance

ssure and tidal gauge data - Glacier mass balance and snow depth (ultrasonic ranging) CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 2 of 13 - Soil moisture and carbon flux - UV radiation intensity Nodes communicate via a multi-hop LoRaWAN mesh to six Regional Base Stations (RBS-A through RBS-F), which pre-process raw streams and forward aggregated da

pre-process raw streams and forward aggregated data to a Central Cloud Platform. Authorised researchers, government agencies, and NGOs access the platform via a web dashboard, REST API, or mobile alerts. The system supports two data acquisition paradigms - Push-based and Pull-based - applied selectively depending on the urgency and nature of each sensor parameter. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study fo

sensor parameter. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 3 of 13Question 1  Push-Based vs Pull-Based Data Acquisition 1.1 Push-Based Model in CLIMATE SENTINEL In the push-based model, sensor nodes autonomously transmit data to the Regional Base Station without waiting for a request. A node continuously monitors its measured values against pre

tinuously monitors its measured values against pre- configured thresholds. When a significant deviation is detected - such as a sudden 8 ppm jump in CO₂ or a 3 °C temperature spike within 10 minutes - the node immediately transmits a data packet containing its node ID, timestamp, all current readings, and an event-type flag. Working Steps (Push): 1.Sensor node CS-47 (Amazon Rainforest Zone) sample

.Sensor node CS-47 (Amazon Rainforest Zone) samples air quality every 500 ms. 2.Onboard microcontroller compares the reading to baseline thresholds stored in EEPROM. 3.If AQI exceeds 150 (unhealthy level) or CO₂ rises > 5 ppm/min, the node transitions from sleep to active radio mode. 4.A JSON payload is assembled: { node_id, timestamp, co2_ppm, aqi, temp_C, lat, lon, event:'AQI_SPIKE' }. 5.The pay

, temp_C, lat, lon, event:'AQI_SPIKE' }. 5.The payload is transmitted via LoRa to the nearest mesh relay, which forwards it hop-by- hop to RBS-B (Manaus, Brazil). 6.RBS-B validates, timestamps, and stores the packet, then triggers an automated ALERT to registered emergency responders and uploads data to the Central Cloud. 7.The web dashboard updates in near-real-time (< 2 seconds end-to-end latenc

s in near-real-time (< 2 seconds end-to-end latency). Climate Use Case for Push: - Wildfire early warning - sudden AQI + temperature spike at CS-89 (Australia Outback) pushes an alert to fire services within 2 seconds. - Glacial lake outburst flood (GLOF) detection - rapid pressure change at CS-12 (Himalayas) triggers immediate push to downstream villages. - Coastal storm surge - tidal gauge cross

illages. - Coastal storm surge - tidal gauge crossing 2 m sends emergency push to coastal authority. 1.2 Pull-Based Model in CLIMATE SENTINEL In the pull-based model, sensor nodes remain in low-power sleep state. Data is collected only when an authorised user or an automated scheduled process sends a query to the Regional Base Station. The base station then wakes the relevant nodes via a downlink

tion then wakes the relevant nodes via a downlink command, collects readings, and returns the data to the requester. Working Steps (Pull): 8.Dr. Kavya Rao (glaciologist, IISc Bengaluru) logs into the Climate Sentinel Portal and selects nodes CS-01-CS-15 (Arctic, Greenland). CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 4 of 139.She issues a pull quer

a Acquisition Page 4 of 139.She issues a pull query: 'Retrieve glacier mass balance and surface albedo for the past 30 days.' 10.The query is routed to RBS-A (Reykjavik, Iceland) with parameters: node_range=CS-01:15, params=[glacier_area, albedo, temp], time_range=past_30d. 11.RBS-A sends a wakeup beacon over LoRa downlink to nodes CS-01-CS-15. 12.Nodes wake, compile their stored sensor logs, and

Nodes wake, compile their stored sensor logs, and transmit consolidated payloads to RBS- A. 13.RBS-A aggregates readings, runs anomaly detection, and generates a structured DataReport. 14.The report is returned to Dr. Rao's dashboard as an interactive chart within ~45 seconds, and is saved to her research repository. Climate Use Case for Pull: - Monthly deforestation index - researcher pulls NDVI-

nthly deforestation index - researcher pulls NDVI-correlated humidity and CO₂ data from Amazon nodes for trend analysis. - Ocean pH sampling - marine biologist requests weekly pull from coastal buoy sensors to track ocean acidification. - IPCC Report preparation - bulk pull query covering 10 years of baseline temperature across all zones for regression modelling. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for

sion modelling. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 5 of 13Question 2  Comparison: Push vs Pull Models The following table compares both models across five parameters with specific reference to the CLIMATE SENTINEL system: Parameter Push Model Pull Model Push  Climate AdvantagePull  Climate Advantage Data TransferContinuous / event-drive

te Advantage Data TransferContinuous / event-drivenOn-demand / scheduledInstant alerts on sudden CO₂ spikes or wildfire smoke surgesControlled data requests reduce redundant transmissions Network TrafficHigh during anomaly eventsLow; only when queriedNecessary for real- time flood / storm surge warningsEfficient for periodic glacier retreat surveys Latency Near-zero (milliseconds)Moderate (seconds

Latency Near-zero (milliseconds)Moderate (seconds-minutes)Critical for heatwave early-warning and evacuation triggersAcceptable for monthly deforestation trend reports Energy UsageHigher (always- on radio)Lower (sleep-wake cycles)Justified where renewable solar/wind powers sensor nodesPreferred in remote Arctic stations with limited power Use Cases Wildfire, flood, storm surgeOcean pH, soil carbo

s Wildfire, flood, storm surgeOcean pH, soil carbon, ice coresReal-time disaster response and emergency managementLong-term climate baseline data collection Summary: Neither model is universally superior. CLIMATE SENTINEL uses a hybrid adaptive strategy: push for life-critical and time-sensitive climate events, pull for research-grade, historical, and low-urgency data acquisition. CLIMATE SENTINEL

and low-urgency data acquisition. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 6 of 13Question 3  EER Diagram Description Since a graphical EER diagram is produced as a separate diagram artefact, this section provides a structured textual description of all entities, attributes, relationships, and cardinalities for the CLIMATE SENTINEL EER model. E

ardinalities for the CLIMATE SENTINEL EER model. Entity Relationships Cardinality ClimateNode belongs to SensorNetwork; generates ClimateReading; raises AlertMany nodes → 1 network; 1 node → many readings; 1 node → many alerts SensorNetwork connects to BaseStation; contains ClimateNodesMany networks → 1 base station; 1 network → many nodes BaseStation receives data from SensorNetwork; responds to

ion receives data from SensorNetwork; responds to Query1 station ← many networks; 1 station → many queries ClimateReading produced by ClimateNode; included in DataReportMany readings → 1 node; many readings → 1 report Alert triggered by ClimateReading; notifies UserMany alerts → 1 reading; many alerts → many users User/Researcher submits Query; receives DataReport; subscribes to Alert1 user → many

eives DataReport; subscribes to Alert1 user → many queries; 1 user ← many reports Query sent to BaseStation; produces DataReportMany queries → 1 station; 1 query → 1 report DataReport contains ClimateReadings; delivered to User1 report → many readings; 1 report → 1 user Specialisation / Generalisation (EER-Specific Features) - ClimateNode is a generalised entity. It is specialised into: Atmospheri

ralised entity. It is specialised into: AtmosphericNode (measures CO₂, AQI, CH₄), HydrologicalNode (measures sea level, tidal gauge, soil moisture), and GlacialNode (measures ice mass, albedo, snow depth). Specialisation is OVERLAPPING and PARTIAL. - User is specialised into Researcher (has institution, field_of_study), GovernmentAgency (has jurisdiction, authority_level), and NGO (has ngo_registr

iction, authority_level), and NGO (has ngo_registration, region_focus). Specialisation is DISJOINT and TOTAL. Weak Entity - ClimateReading is a weak entity - it is uniquely identified only by the combination of (node_id + timestamp). It has a total participation relationship with ClimateNode (its identifying entity). - Alert is a weak entity dependent on ClimateReading for identification. CLIMATE

ent on ClimateReading for identification. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 7 of 13Aggregation - The relationship between Query, BaseStation, and ClimateReading is modelled through aggregation: a Query is an abstraction over the (BaseStation - responds-with → ClimateReading) relationship, and DataReport is associated with this aggregated

and DataReport is associated with this aggregated unit. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 8 of 13Question 4  Database Attributes for Main Entities The following section details the complete attribute set required to store CLIMATE SENTINEL sensor data in a relational database. Primary keys (PK) and foreign keys (FK) are indicated. Entity

(PK) and foreign keys (FK) are indicated. Entity Key Attributes Description ClimateNode (Sensor)node_id (PK), node_type, latitude, longitude, deployment_altitude, battery_level, solar_charge_rate, firmware_version, last_calibration_date, statusPhysical sensor hardware deployed across climate zones SensorNetwork network_id (PK), network_name, region, topology_type, communication_protocol, encrypti

n, topology_type, communication_protocol, encryption_key, gateway_count, coverage_radius_kmWireless mesh network connecting all sensor nodes BaseStation station_id (PK), station_name, location, processing_capacity, data_storage_tb, uptime_percent, satellite_uplink_bandwidth, solar_powered (bool)Regional hub for data aggregation and pre- processing ClimateReading reading_id (PK), node_id (FK), time

ClimateReading reading_id (PK), node_id (FK), timestamp, temperature_C, co2_ppm, humidity_percent, air_quality_index, sea_level_change_mm, glacier_area_km2, methane_ppb, uv_index, acquisition_modeIndividual sensor measurement record Alert alert_id (PK), reading_id (FK), alert_type, severity_level, threshold_exceeded, triggered_at, resolved_at, notified_agenciesAutomated warning generated on thresh

fied_agenciesAutomated warning generated on threshold breach User / Researcheruser_id (PK), name, institution, role, email, access_level, region_of_interest, api_keyScientists, policymakers, or NGOs accessing the data Query query_id (PK), user_id (FK), station_id (FK), query_type, parameters_requested, time_range_start, time_range_end, submitted_at, response_time_ms, statusData request issued by a

, response_time_ms, statusData request issued by a user to the base station DataReport report_id (PK), query_id (FK), generated_at, format_type, file_size_kb, anomalies_detected, ai_summaryProcessed and analysed output delivered to the user Additional Notes on Database Design - Indexing: composite index on (node_id, timestamp) in ClimateReading for time-series range queries. - Partitioning: Climat

time-series range queries. - Partitioning: ClimateReading table partitioned by region and month for scalable storage. - TimescaleDB or InfluxDB: recommended for hypertable storage of high-frequency sensor time series. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 9 of 13 - Normalisation: The schema is in 3NF. Node location data is stored once in Cli

s in 3NF. Node location data is stored once in ClimateNode, not duplicated in readings. - Audit fields: created_at, updated_at, and deleted_at (soft delete) fields should be added to all entities. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 10 of 13Question 5  System Architecture Description The CLIMATE SENTINEL architecture follows a five-tier hi

IMATE SENTINEL architecture follows a five-tier hierarchical model. A separate architecture diagram is produced as a visual artefact; this section provides the detailed textual specification. Tier 1  Sensor Node Layer - 120 heterogeneous sensor nodes (CS-01 to CS-120) across six climate zones. - Hardware: ARM Cortex-M4 MCU, LoRa SX1276 radio, solar panel + LiPo battery, SD card for local bufferin

r panel + LiPo battery, SD card for local buffering. - Firmware: FreeRTOS with interrupt-driven sampling; adaptive duty cycle based on battery level. - Communication: LoRaWAN Class A (uplink dominant) for pull; Class C (always-on receive) for push alerts. Tier 2  Mesh Relay Layer - 30 relay nodes forming a multi-hop LoRa mesh to extend coverage in remote areas. - No processing - purely transparen

remote areas. - No processing - purely transparent forwarding with forward error correction (FEC). Tier 3  Regional Base Station Layer (RBS) - 6 Regional Base Stations: RBS-A (Arctic), RBS-B (Amazon), RBS-C (Himalayas), RBS-D (Sub-Saharan Africa), RBS-E (Pacific Coast), RBS-F (Urban Asia). - Hardware: Raspberry Pi 5 cluster + LoRa gateway + 4G/satellite uplink. - Functions: data validation, outl

tellite uplink. - Functions: data validation, outlier detection, compression, alert generation, local 7-day cache, pull request routing. Tier 4  Central Cloud Platform - Multi-region deployment (AWS / Azure) with 99.99% SLA. - Microservices: Ingestion Service, Alert Engine, Query Processor, AI Analytics Engine, Report Generator. - AI/ML: LSTM models for temperature anomaly prediction; Random Fore

ls for temperature anomaly prediction; Random Forest for wildfire risk scoring; time-series forecasting for glacier melt projections. - Storage: TimescaleDB for time-series, PostgreSQL for relational data, S3 for raw data archive. - Message queue: Apache Kafka for real-time stream processing at 50,000 events/second peak. Tier 5  User Interface / Application Layer CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for

pplication Layer CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 11 of 13 - Web Dashboard: interactive map with live sensor overlays, historical trend charts, threshold configuration. - REST API: OAuth2-secured endpoints for programmatic data access by research institutions. - Mobile App: push notification delivery for emergency alerts; offline caching

ion delivery for emergency alerts; offline caching of recent data. - GIS Integration: ArcGIS / QGIS plugin for spatial analysis of climate data. - IPCC-compatible Export: automated NetCDF and CSV exports formatted to WMO standards. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 12 of 13Question 6  Recommended Model for Real-Time Climate Monitoring Re

ommended Model for Real-Time Climate Monitoring Recommendation: Adaptive Hybrid Model (Push-Dominant) For real-time climate monitoring in a crisis-era context, the Push-Based Model is the primary recommendation, augmented by a Pull-Based mechanism for research and historical queries. This is justified by the following arguments: Justification for Push as Primary - Zero-latency crisis response: Cli

ush as Primary - Zero-latency crisis response: Climate disasters such as wildfires, floods, and heatwaves develop within minutes. A push model ensures sensor readings reach emergency management systems in under 2 seconds  a delay that can directly save lives. Pull-based systems introduce mandatory round-trip latency that is unacceptable in these scenarios. - Event-driven efficiency: Modern push i

cenarios. - Event-driven efficiency: Modern push implementations are not always-on; they use adaptive threshold-based triggering. Nodes sleep until a significant change is detected, then push. This makes push nearly as energy-efficient as pull in stable conditions while retaining instant responsiveness. - Irreversibility of climate events: Unlike network service outages that can be remedied, clima

etwork service outages that can be remedied, climate events  a glacial lake outburst, a Category 5 storm, a methane blowout  cannot be reversed. The asymmetric cost of missing an event justifies the slightly higher energy use of push. - Autonomous operation: In remote zones (deep Amazon, Arctic tundra), human- initiated pull queries are impossible if communication links fail. Push nodes with sto

e if communication links fail. Push nodes with store-and-forward capability deliver data autonomously even during partial network outages. Role of Pull Model - Pull remains essential for: scheduled low-frequency measurements (monthly glacier surveys, seasonal carbon flux audits), bandwidth-constrained links (satellite-only nodes in remote areas), and bulk historical data retrieval for IPCC reports

nd bulk historical data retrieval for IPCC reports and climate modelling. The Hybrid Strategy in CLIMATE SENTINEL CLIMATE SENTINEL classifies every sensor parameter into one of two acquisition policies: - PUSH parameters: CO₂ spike > 5 ppm/min, AQI > 150, temperature rate-of-change > 2 °C/10 min, sea level > threshold, tidal surge, wildfire smoke index. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment

ndex. CLIMATE SENTINEL  Case Study for Assignment Wireless Sensor Networks & Data Acquisition Page 13 of 13 - PULL parameters: monthly glacier mass balance, weekly ocean pH, daily soil carbon flux, annual deforestation indices, baseline humidity and UV records. This hybrid design achieves the optimal balance: real-time safety monitoring through push, with energy-efficient research data collection
sh, with energy-efficient research data collection through pull. It represents the gold standard for 21st- century climate monitoring infrastructure. - End of Case Study -
